coredata.dc:title: PVDF-based molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent test paper with improved visualization effect for catechol monitoring
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Ting
Xu, Yeqing
Meng, Minjia
Li, Chunxiang
coredata.dc:description: Various methods have been proposed for the determination of catechol (CT), however, the actual applications of these methods were restricted by instrument dependence, complex operations and difficulty of the on-site monitoring. In this work, a molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent test paper based on hydrophobic polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane was successfully prepared for facile and on-site monitoring of CT. Firstly, a uniform core–shell molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor (CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs) was successfully synthesized. Then the test paper was fabricated by facilely coating CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs on a PVDF membrane by the vacuum filtration method. Ultimately, under optimal conditions, CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs exhibited excellent stability and a relatively lower limit of detection (LOD) of 0.11 μM, satisfied recovery rates of 97.73%-102.30% and relative standard deviations of 2.01%-2.34%. Moreover, the selective study and ions inference experiment synergistically proved that the polymer has good anti-interference ability. Afterwards, the PVDF membrane was eventually selected to be the optimal substrate due to the uniform distribution of fluorescent substances on its surface, thus, greatly improved the accuracy of detection. The resultant test paper hugely improved the practicability and provided a reference for water quality testing. The facile and efficient strategy reported here opens a window for the rapid, visual and on-site detection of CT in river water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Fluorescent test paper
Visual detection
Catechol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of fluorescent visualization test paper
Determination of actual river water samples
Optimization of the detection conditions of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs
Analytical performance of the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs
Selection of substrate for fluorescent test paper
The fluorescent test paper for visual detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21808089 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2019M661750 , 2019T120400 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20190245 ), Major projects of education department of Jiangsu province (No.17KJA530003), Programs of Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No.15JDG024).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and SEM images of CdTe@SiO2 (a, d), CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs (b, e) and CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs (c, f); STEM images of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs (g).

Fluorescence spectra and the corresponding linearities of the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs (a, c) and CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs (b, d) upon the exposure to 0–100 µM CT. (inset: corresponding fluorescence color images under the 365 nm UV lamp).
The flow chart of preparation of PVDF-based fluorescent test paper (a); Photos of the PVDF-based fluorescent test paper dripped with 0–100 μM CT solutions (b).
Schematic synthetic illustration of the preparation of the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs.
Results for the determination of CT in river water samples by fluorescence analysis method and HPLC method.

head.ce:title: PVDF-based molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent test paper with improved visualization effect for catechol monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Advanced Chemical Engineering Laboratory of Green Materials and Energy of Jiangsu Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
Advanced Chemical Engineering Laboratory of Green Materials and Energy of Jiangsu Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Advanced Chemical Engineering Laboratory of Green Materials and Energy of Jiangsu Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Various methods have been proposed for the determination of catechol (CT), however, the actual applications of these methods were restricted by instrument dependence, complex operations and difficulty of the on-site monitoring. In this work, a molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent test paper based on hydrophobic polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane was successfully prepared for facile and on-site monitoring of CT. Firstly, a uniform core–shell molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor (CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs) was successfully synthesized. Then the test paper was fabricated by facilely coating CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs on a PVDF membrane by the vacuum filtration method. Ultimately, under optimal conditions, CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs exhibited excellent stability and a relatively lower limit of detection (LOD) of 0.11 μM, satisfied recovery rates of 97.73%-102.30% and relative standard deviations of 2.01%-2.34%. Moreover, the selective study and ions inference experiment synergistically proved that the polymer has good anti-interference ability. Afterwards, the PVDF membrane was eventually selected to be the optimal substrate due to the uniform distribution of fluorescent substances on its surface, thus, greatly improved the accuracy of detection. The resultant test paper hugely improved the practicability and provided a reference for water quality testing. The facile and efficient strategy reported here opens a window for the rapid, visual and on-site detection of CT in river water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Catechol (CT) is a representative phenolic compound with an ortho-hydroxyl structure and is widely used in dyes, flavoring agents, cosmetics, pesticides and medicines [1,2]. Even low concentrations of CT can pose a threat to human health and the environment [3,4]. For example, after entering the bloodstream, CT can adversely affect the liver function of human beings and even induce cancer [5]. Owing to these reasons, CT is regarded as a kind of hazardous environmental pollutant by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [6]. According to the “Integrated Wastewater Discharge Standard”, China Environmental Protection Association (CEPA) has also made clear regulations on the content of phenolic compounds in water, and the concentration of CT in river surface water cannot exceed 0.1 mg/L (0.91 μM). So far, gas chromatography [7], electrochemistry [8], UV spectrometry [9], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10] and fluorescence method [11] have been used to detect CT. Among them, the fluorescence method has attracted wide attention owing to its low cost, easy operation, rapidity and high sensitivity [12]. However, the lack of selectivity severely limits its application scope. Hence, it is of great significance and promising to quest a fluorescent measurement that could sensitively and selectively detect CT in complex environment.

Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) is dedicated to synthesizing molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) that match the target in structure and size [13–15]. The as-formed MIPs possess greenificated imprinting process and high selectivity [16–18]. Thus, the fluorescence MIPs which combines the ultra-sensitivity of fluorescence analysis and high selectivity of MIPs is an ideal option for target detection in complex environment [19]. However, traditional MIPs are usually constructed based on non-covalent bond interactions (such as hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, and van der Waals forces, etc.), but this non-covalent bond force is usually weak and easily broken during the polymerization process, resulting in the poor selectivity of MIPs. In recent years, many researchers have devoted themselves to the study of improving the selectivity of MIPs, and found that the covalent bond based on boron affinity can solve the problem of poor selectivity of traditional MIPs [20].

From a practical point of view, it is of great significance to develop solid sensors for portable and on-site detection of target. The fluorescent test paper has recently been developed owing to its excellent merits of on-site visual recognition capability to detect the target by the naked eyes [21]. However, compared with single emission fluorescence sensor [22,23], the ratiometric fluorescence sensor can provide rich color evolution, which is easier for eyes to catch and distinguish than the brightness variation of a single color [24–27]. Generally speaking, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor with reference fluorescence and target sensitive substances can avoid some interference factors of fluorescence detection, including background interference, operating parameter, instrument fluctuation, etc. [28,29]. In general, the test paper is prepared by loading fluorescent sensors on the micro-porous membrane or filter paper [21]. Up to date, many previous literature have been reported the use of test paper with fluorescence sensor [30,31]. Although the methods above can realize the visual detection of the target analyte, it inevitably has problems with the uneven diffusion of solution and the presence of background color under the UV lamp. Furthermore, the “coffee ring effect” on the test strip will affect the detection effect of the target [32,33]. Yet compared to different hydrophilic and hydrophobic membranes and filter paper, hydrophobic polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane has some merits: (1) the roughness of the membrane surface is conducive for the loading of fluorescent sensors; (2) there is no other background color under the UV lamp, making the detection result more accurate and legible; (3) the PVDF membrane has low-cost and good mechanical properties [34], which is beneficial to actual storage and testing.

Herein, a facile fluorescent test paper based on a ratiometric fluorescent sensor (CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs) was designed for on-site detection of CT in river water. Specifically, silica microsphere capped CdTe QDs (CdTe@SiO2) was prepared as internal standard, Si QDs was used as a response signal, the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was successfully fabricated by precipitation polymerization and applied to detect CT in river water. Because of the specificity of CT structure, 4-vinylphenylboronic acid (VPBA) was selected to be functional monomer of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs. Furthermore, CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was attached to the hydrophobic PVDF membrane to obtain a fluorescent test paper by the vacuum filtration method, which not only guaranteed the load of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs, but also improved the visual detection effect of the test paper. And it was further verified that the test paper could be used to detect CT in the actual river water samples. Thus, this work provides a practical guidance for further design and application of fluorescent test papers.
The materials (1.1. Experimental materials) and instruments (1.2. Instruments) used in the experiment can be found in the supporting information.

Initially, trisodium citrate dehydrate (SC) (1.0 g) was dispersed in distilled water (30 mL) for about 20 min under nitrogen atmosphere, then 1.0 mL of (3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS) was dissolved by stirring homogeneously up to 30 min. The mixture was subsequently allowed to high temperature hydrothermal reaction at 160 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature. The prepared blue Si QDs were washed by acetonitrile to remove large particles. After that collected by dialysis through a dialysis membrane (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for 6.0 h and then stored at 4.0 °C for further use.

The synthesis and modification processes of CdTe QDs with 3-(methacryloxyl) propyl trimethoxysilane (KH570) (1.3. Synthesis of the CdTe QDs) and CdTe@SiO2 (1.4. Preparation and modification of CdTe@SiO2 ) can be found in the supporting information.

First of all, the CdTe@SiO2-KH570 (50 mg) and Si QDs (1.0 mL) were dispersed in ethanol (60 mL). Next, the target CT (11.0 mg), monomer VPBA (9.47 mg) and crosslinking agent ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) (132 µL) were added into the above mixture. After blowing nitrogen for 20 min, initiator azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) (10 mg) was added subsequently. Then, the system was sealed and prepolymerized for 6.0 h at 50 °C and then heated to 60 °C for 24 h under continuously stirring. At last, the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was attained by centrifugation and washed repeatedly with ethanol and distilled water. After that, the products were eluted with a prepared solution of methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) to remove CT by Soxhlet extractor for 2.0 days. The counterparts, the preparation way of non-imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensors (CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs) was similar to the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs except the addition of CT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fluorescent visualization test paper
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of fluorescent test paper can be found in the supporting information (1.5. Fabrication of fluorescent visualization test paper).

The measurement of fluorescence intensity was scanned by the fluorescent spectrophotometer (the excitation wavelength was 360 nm). All the fluorescent experiments were performed at room temperature. Meanwhile, CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was dispersed in the volumetric flask to obtain the stock solution (2.0 mg/mL) and different concentrations (0–100 μmol/L) of CT solution were prepared respectively. The stock solution (100 µL) was placed in a 10 mL calibrated test tube after that, added to the above different concentrations of CT respectively. Subsequently, the mixture was diluted with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 0.01 mol/L) to the mark and mixed thoroughly. Afterwards, the mixture was incubated for a reasonable time at room temperature and transferred into the quartz cell to take fluorescent detection. For selectivity and anti-interference experiments, resorcinol (RS), hydroquinone (HQ), bisphenol a (BPA), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), 4-methylcatechol (4-MC), dopamine (DA), gallic acid (GA) and other interfering ions were detected under the same concentration by the similar procedure described above. The added concentration of analogs and interfering ions was 60 μmol/L. In order to carry out the visual detection, all of the tubes were put under a UV lamp with 365 nm wavelength, and the photos were taken in darkness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of actual river water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of river water samples (1.6. Determination of CT in water samples) and parameter settings for HPLC analysis (1.7. HPLC analysis of actual samples) can be found in the supporting information.

The synthesis procedure of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was shown in Scheme 1 . Two types QDs with different color emissions (i.e. red and blue) were combined by molecular imprinting technique to design the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs with specific CT recognition abilities and visual detection properties. Specifically, the red CdTe QDs, as the standard internal material, were embedded in silica nanocores to obtain CdTe@SiO2 via the sol–gel method. After that, the blue Si QDs were joined in the reaction as a response signal. The functional monomer (VPBA) and target (CT) were formed covalent bonds between boronic acid functional groups and vicinal diols. The CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was successfully synthesized by the addition of crosslinking agent (EGDMA) and evocating agent (AIBN). Due to the presence of CT, the fluorescence of Si QDs was quenched, however, the fluorescence of CdTe QDs was protected by silica layer. Correspondingly, the fluorescence of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs has been changed from blue to magenta under the UV lamp. After eluting the template of CT, the imprinted layer was obtained the specific binding sites of shape, size and functional groups with CT. At the same time, the fluorescence of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs has showed blue due to the recovery of Si QDs.

The average particle size distributions of Si QDs and CdTe QDs were approximately 3.86 nm and 4.6 nm in the insets of Figure S1a and S1b, respectively. Figure S1c and S1d displayed the emission spectra of Si QDs at different excitation wavelengths, which indicated that Si QDs possessed the optimal emission peak when excited at 360 nm. Then, the morphological structures of CdTe@SiO2, CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs and CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs were characterized by TEM and SEM techniques. Fig. 1 a and 1d showed that CdTe@SiO2 nanoparticles possessed good dispersion and smooth surface. The average diameter of them was about 832 nm (Figure S3a). TEM and SEM images of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs (Fig. 1b and 1e) and CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs (Fig. 1c and 1f) showed that the two nanoparticles were spherical and the imprinted layers were observed around the CdTe@SiO2 and the surface became rough after the imprinting polymerization. Moreover, the synthesized CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs and CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs nanoparticles have uniform particle sizes and their average diameters were about 930 nm and 928 nm, respectively (Figure S3b and S3c), and the thickness of imprinted layers were about 50 nm. Afterwards, scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM) with the elemental distribution mapping (Fig. 1g) and laser scanning confocal microscope (LCSM) (Figure S2) jointly displayed that the CdTe QDs and Si QDs were successfully embedded in the silica core and the imprinted layer, respectively.

The as-prepared CdTe QDs, CdTe@SiO2, CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs and CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs revealed the different functional groups and were verified by using the FT-IR spectroscopy (Figure S4). The broadband at 3429 cm−1 corresponded to O–H stretching vibration on the surface of Si QDs. The peaks at 1627 cm−1 and 1631 cm−1 were due to C = O bending vibration from carboxyl [35], which indicated the presence of TGA-capped CdTe QDs. The characteristic peak at 2975 cm−1 was attributed to C–H stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 1729 cm−1 was assigned to C = O stretching vibration. The broad peaks at 1091 cm−1, 793 cm−1 and 467 cm−1 were revealed Si-O-Si asymmetric vibration and Si-O symmetric bending, respectively [36]. The characteristic peak at 1453 cm−1 represented the N–H bending vibration. And the spectra exhibited absorption band at 1386 cm−1, which could be related to B-O stretching vibration [37]. In general, these results indicated the successful preparation of the imprinted fluorescence nanoparticles.

As shown in Figure S5b, UV–vis of Si QDs exhibited a clear absorption peak at about 331 nm, considered as the n-π* transition. When excitation wavelength at 360 nm, Si QDs exhibited a strong blue fluorescence with the emission wavelength at 447 nm. Meanwhile, as shown in Figure S5a, CdTe QDs shown a strong red fluorescence with an emission wavelength at 653 nm, and the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs possessed two distinct emission bands at 447 nm and 653 nm, which were ascribed the constituent parts of Si QDs and CdTe QDs. To sum up, these results have showed that CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the detection conditions of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to get better experimental results, some important factors needed to be determined and optimized, such as optical stability, pH value and response time, which provided a basis for the next steps of fluorescent and visual detection.

To our knowledge, the optical stability of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs played a vital role in fluorescent and visual detection. Before establishing an analysis method, it was necessary to study its optical stability. As could be seen from Figure S6, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I653/I447) existed with no obvious changes in 120 min. Based on the research, it could be found that CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs had good stability and could be stably detected throughout the process of CT analysis.

It was played a significant part for pH value in the fluorescent detection system. As shown in Figure S7, the effect of pH value on CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs ranged from 3.0 to 13 before (black line) and after (red line) exposure to 50 µM CT was explored under the same test conditions. It could be seen that CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs had a relatively strong fluorescence intensity in the neutral environment. And the fluorescence intensity was relatively weak in over-acidic and over-alkaline environments. From the gap between black line and red line, when the pH value was around 7.0, the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs possessed the best recognition effect on CT. As we known that the pH value of 7.4 was the optimal condition for the interaction of the boric acid group and cis-diol-containing molecules [9]. The results were consistent with those reported in the literature. Thus, pH = 7.4 was adopted for subsequent experiments.

To a certain extent, the influence of the response time between CT and CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was investigated. As it was shown in Figure S8, the I653/I447 value of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs with CT was calculated at different interval times. For the first 10 min, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs decreased rapidly, and after that, it reached equilibrium. Moreover, when CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was incubated in a CT solution (100 µM), the fluorescence conspicuously decreased at 447 nm. Meanwhile, the corresponding fluorescent color gradually changed from blue to magenta under a 365 nm UV lamp (Figure S9). Therefore, 10 min was selected as the optimal time for the further fluorescent tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity and visual effect of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs to CT were further evaluated by analyzing the fluorescent emission spectra and the corresponding color changes under a 365 nm UV lamp. In the absence of CT, CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs possessed two distinct emission peaks at 447 nm and 653 nm, respectively, which due to the presence of Si QDs and CdTe QDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, with the increase of CT concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs gradually reduced at 447 nm, while the fluorescence intensity constantly remained at 653 nm. By calculating the fluorescence intensity ratio under different concentrations of CT, a linear curve was obtained. Therefore, in the concentration range of 0–100 µM, the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs could be used to detect CT. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I447/I653 ) and the concentration of CT (C CT) could be showed by the following equation: I447/I653 = 1.501–0.0139C CT (R2 = 0.9940). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to 0.11 µM by 3δ/k, where δ was the relative standard deviation of blank measurement, k represented the slope of the calibration curve (Fig. 2c). In addition, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 2a, with the increase of CT concentrations, the continuous variation of fluorescence colors from blue to magenta could be observed by the naked eyes. Thus, CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was feasible to visually detect CT with naked eyes.

In order to confirm that the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs exhibited much stronger fluorescence quenching than CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs (i.e., non-imprinted nanoparticles). The CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs was prepared as comparisons and the fluorescence intensities were detected exposed to the same concentration range. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs at 447 nm also gradually decreased in a relatively narrow range while the one at 653 nm constantly remained upon the increase of CT concentrations. The linear curve of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs was satisfied with an equation of I447/I653 = 1.482–0.00505C CT (R2 = 0.9890) (Fig. 2d). The imprinting factor (IF) (IF = kMIP/kNIP ) [38] was calculated to 2.75. And the corresponding color changes of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs were showed in the inset in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence color changes were not obvious by naked eyes compared with CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs even at a relatively high CT concentration. This phenomenon could be mainly explained that the lack of specific imprinting recognition sites in CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs, and CT could not effectively interact with internal Si QDs, just leading to very weak fluctuations in the fluorescence intensity and color changes. In conclusion, the results have showed that CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs owned better CT binding capacity and the greater potential for visual detection.

To further verify the selective recognition ability and anti-interference ability of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs in practical detection, seven structurally related analogs (i.e., RS, HQ, BPA, OPD, 4-MC, DA, GA) (Figure S10) and several possible coexisting ions (i.e., Zn2+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe2+) in the river water were specifically investigated under the same conditions.

As shown in Figure S11a, the selective experiment of fluorescence quenching effect on CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs and CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs exposed to the same concentration of analogs. It could be found that the fluorescence intensity ratio (I447/I653) value of the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was about 0.695 by CT (C = 60 µM), which was obviously lower than other analogs. The results demonstated that CT had greater influence on CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs than other analogs. Afterwards, the selectivity of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs was also studied. The I447/I653 valuewas higher than CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs. This phenomenon could be explained that the presence of many imprinted sites which matched with CT in CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs and could successfully rebind with CT.

Furthermore, it could be clearly seen in Figure S11b that CT possessed the most obvious quenching change on CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs, and the I447/I653 value after adding coexisting ions had almost no effect in interference experiment. Meanwhile, the insets of Figure S11a and S11b displayed the obvious color variation of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs after injecting 60 µM CT. These results implied that CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs possessed an excellent recognition ability of CT over other analogs and interfering ions in actual river detection.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CT toward Si QDs was investigated. In general, the known possible fluorescence quenching mechanisms could be generally divided into the dynamic quenching effect (DQE), static quenching effect (SQE), internal filtration effect (IFE), electron transfer (ET) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [39]. It was clear in Figure S12a that after adding the CT, the UV absorption positions of Si QDs and CT were not obviously shift, which was indicated that the quenching mechanism couldn’t be SQE and IFE. And the overlapping space was not observed remarkable between the UV–vis spectrum of CT and the fluorescence excitation spectrum (pink curve) and emission spectrum (green curve) of Si QDs. It could be judged that the quenching mechanism also couldn’t be FRET. In addition, the zeta potentials of Si QDs (-2.21 mV) and CT (+12.91 mV) were detected respectively, as shown in Figure S12b. Obviously, the Si QDs were not electron-rich quantum dots. Results verified that the ET was unlikely to be happened in this study. As demonstrated in Figure S12c and S12d, the changes of the fluorescence lifetime of Si QDs in the presence and absence of CT were studied. After linear fitting, obvious changes of fluorescence lifetime were observed for Si QDs (τave = 8.585 ns) and Si QDs with CT (τave = 3.992 ns) (τ0/τ1 ≠ 1). After studying the results above, it could be concluded that the main quenching mechanism of Si QDs toward CT was DQE with a high probability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of substrate for fluorescent test paper
ce:section.ce:para: The visual effect of fluorescent test paper could be affected by many factors. For example, the selection of substrate material would affect the diffusion rate of the polymer and the target solutions on the fluorescent test paper, thereby affect the visual detection effect. Moreover, the preparation method of the test paper would also directly affect the loading amount of polymers on the test paper.

Firstly, the differences between PVDF membrane, filter paper, polyethylene (PE) membrane, fibreglass (BF) membrane, regenerated cellulose (RC) membrane and quartz membrane were studied. As shown in Figure S13, the PVDF membrane and other substrates both displayed white colors in the bright field, and it could be clearly seen that the surfaces of the PVDF membrane and PE membrane were obviously smoother than others. Furthermore, the filter paper and PE membrane could be observed blue and deep violet background under UV lamp, while other substrates presented similar to purple, respectively. Next, CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was coated on the different substrates by vacuum filtration method. After dripping CT solution (10 µL), the visualized photos of the different substrates could be observed under the UV lamp. As shown in Figure S14, the blue background of filter paper was similar to the blue Si QDs and PE membrane with deep violet background also exhibited a deeper color variation at the same condition under the UV lamp. Moreover, the PE membrane with too smooth surface, which was not conducive to the loading of the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs. And it was also found that the filter paper and other membranes both observed uneven color distribution. This might be attribute to that the material of other substrates except for the PVDF membrane possessed too rough or too smooth surface, which was not beneficial for the uniform diffusion of the solution and the strong load of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs. Thus, the PVDF membrane was chosen to be the optimal fluorescent test paper substrate.

The good mechanical properties were facilitated the storage and carrying of fluorescent test paper. Afterwards, the tensile strength performances of different substrates were explored, separately. As shown in Figure S15, these substrates loaded with the same amount of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs nanoparticles showed significant differences in tensile strength. The tensile strength of PVDF membrane and PE membrane was higher than other substrates, which further indicated that PVDF membrane was conducive to storage and testing in practical application.

In order to further explore the visual effect of hydrophobic and hydrophilic PVDF membrane, respectively. Due to colorless and transparent Si QDs under the bright field, the red CdTe QDs were selected to intuitively and clearly observe the visual effect of the hydrophilic and hydrophobic PVDF membrane. Firstly, a drop of the red CdTe QDs solution was added on the hydrophobic and hydrophilic PVDF membrane respectively. As shown in Figure S16, after dripping CdTe QDs, the surface of the hydrophilic PVDF membrane formed a ring because of the uneven diffusion rate of the solution on the membrane surface. However, the CdTe QDs appeared as a droplet on the surface of the hydrophobic PVDF membrane, and the surface still showed a bright red-emission light after being wiped off the droplet. These results indicated that the visual effect of the hydrophobic PVDF membrane could be better than hydrophilic membrane, and CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs could be firmly loaded on the membrane surface by the vacuum filtration method.

In summary, the hydrophobic PVDF membrane with a low-cost, smooth surface, good mechanical property and without other background color was selected as the optimal substrate for the test paper in this experiment. Importantly, the test paper with good visualization and practicality could be used to judge whether the CT in river water was normal or excessive, thus showing the promise for on-site and simple monitoring in the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescent test paper for visual detection
ce:section.ce:para: To simplify the CT detection procedure, the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs was coated on a hydrophobic PVDF membrane to obtain a fluorescent test paper by the vacuum filtration method, which could be easily used for visual detection of CT (the preparation process of fluorescent test paper was showed in Fig. 3 a). To further study this process, the surface morphology of pure PVDF membrane and PVDF test paper was respectively measured by SEM. As expected, there were macroporous structures of the pure PVDF membrane for providing load space to support solid samples (Figure S17a). For the PVDF-based fluorescent test paper, it was found that lots of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs were successfully coated on the PVDF membrane (Figure 17b and S17c). Then, a series of CT solutions, with concentrations ranging from 0 µM to 100 µM in water were prepared. After dripping a drop of CT solution (10 µL) on the test paper (4 mm × 10 mm), as the concentration of CT increased, the fluorescence colors of the test paper gradually changed from blue to magenta, as shown in Fig. 3b. This phenomenon was similar to that of the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs in the solution state, and also showed the fluorescent test paper could be used for the detection of CT in the river water.

The practicability of the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs for CT detection in river water was further investigated via the fluorescence ratiometric method. Due to the low content of CT in river water, the standard recovery method was executed, and the results were exhibited in Table 1 . It clearly illustrated that the obtained recoveries were 97.73%-102.30% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were ranged from 2.01% to 2.34% in river water samples. In the meantime, the CT concentrations have been measured in river water via HPLC method, as shown in Table 1 and Figure S18. The same concentration of CT was detected for three times and the values were basically consist with the found concentration via fluorescence detection method. Hence, the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs could be used for ratio determination of CT levels in river water.

Furthermore, there were many excellent works have been reported to detect CT, recently. Some of them were listed in Table S1 (the first row to the forth row) and compared with the present work. At the same time, some ratiometric fluorescence analysis methods were listed in Table S1 to compare the LODs between the ratiometric fluorescent sensors (the fifth row to the ninth row). It could be concluded in Table S1 that the ratiometric method of this work not only displayed a satisfying detection limit, but also processed good visualization effects for CT among the listed sensors. So, the CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs could be well applied as ideal strategy for sensitive and selective analysis of relevant actual samples.

In summary, a novel and convenient PVDF-based test paper was successfully fabricated for CT detection in river water. The CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs were uniformly loaded on the surface of hydrophobic PVDF membrane by vacuum filtration method, in which the hydrophobic PVDF membrane solved the problem of the non-uniform diffusion of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs leading to the reduction of the visual effect, the application of vacuum filtration not only ensured the polymer load, but also guaranteed the visual detection effect of the test paper. Consequently, the PVDF-based test paper was applied to the on-site visual detection of CT in river water with abundant and obvious color changes. Furthermore, the HPLC analysis method was conducted to verify the accuracy of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence analysis method. Finally, the ratiometric fluorescence analysis method was proved to be an accurate method to detect CT in river, and the PVDF-based test paper was confirmed to be an ultra-convenient detection form with freedom of the sites. Thus, this work provides a bright prospect of fluorescent test paper for the on-site and feasible detection applications in environmental testing.

Ting Huang: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Software. Yeqing Xu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Software, Supervision, Validation. Minjia Meng: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Chunxiang Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos.21808089), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No.2019M661750, 2019T120400), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No.BK20190245), Major projects of education department of Jiangsu province (No.17KJA530003), Programs of Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No.15JDG024).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images of Si QDs and CdTe QDs; the emission spectra of Si QDs at different excitation wavelengths; the size distribution images of CdTe@SiO2, CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs and CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-NIPs; optical stability, pH value and response time of CdTe@SiO2@Si QDs-MIPs; simplified structures of catechol and its analogues; SEM image of membranes; tensile strength of different substrates and so on. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107369.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
